hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **would + V1** (for example, «would spend»).
Explanation: In the result of the second conditional, we use would + base verb.
Example: *If John gave me the money, I would spend it on clothes.*

a1.would spend:
✅ «would spend» — the correct construction would + V1 for the result of the second conditional.
Example: *If John gave me the money, I would spend it on clothes.*
a1.will spend:
❌ «will spend» with will belongs to the first conditional, but here we need would.
Example: *If John gave me the money, I would spend it on clothes.*
a1.spend:
❌ «spend» — Present Simple; in the second conditional result, we describe it through would.
Example: *If John gave me the money, I would spend it on clothes.*
a1.spent:
❌ «spent» — Past Simple; we need would + V1.
Example: *If John gave me the money, I would spend it on clothes.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **if + Present Simple**.
Explanation: The conditional part of type 1 describes a real possibility, so we use Present Simple (for example, the form of «see» for the pronoun «she»).
Example: *Cathy will be upset if she sees Michael at the party.*

a1.sees:
✅ «sees» — the correct form of Present Simple in the subordinate clause for the pronoun «she».
Example: *Cathy will be upset if she sees Michael at the party.*
a1.will see:
❌ «will see» contains will, but in the if-clause of type 1 we do not use will.
Example: *Cathy will be upset if she sees Michael at the party.*
a1.saw:
❌ «saw» — Past Simple; for a real condition, we need Present Simple.
Example: *Cathy will be upset if she sees Michael at the party.*
a1.would see:
❌ «would see» belongs to the second conditional with would, not to the first.
Example: *Cathy will be upset if she sees Michael at the party.*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: The imaginary situation in the second conditional is conveyed by Past Simple of the verb «have».
Example: *Rosemary would buy the house if she had the money.*

a1.had:
✅ «had» — the correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun «she».
Example: *Rosemary would buy the house if she had the money.*
a1.has:
❌ «has» — Present Simple, but the second condition requires Past Simple.
Example: *Rosemary would buy the house if she had the money.*
a1.would have:
❌ «would have» — the result with would, not the part with if.
Example: *Rosemary would buy the house if she had the money.*
a1.have had:
❌ «have had» — the form of Third Conditional (had + V3). It is not used in the second conditional.
Example: *Rosemary would buy the house if she had the money.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **if + Present Simple**.
Explanation: The conditional part of type 1 describes a real possibility, so we use Present Simple (for example, the form of «not want» for the pronoun «I»).
Example: *I won't call him if I don't want to.*

a1.don't want:
✅ «don't want» — the correct form of Present Simple in the subordinate clause for the pronoun «I».
Example: *I won't call him if I don't want to.*
a1.won't want:
❌ «won't want» contains will, but in the if-clause of type 1 we do not use will.
Example: *I won't call him if I don't want to.*
a1.didn't want:
❌ «didn't want» — Past Simple; for a real condition, we need Present Simple.
Example: *I won't call him if I don't want to.*
a1.wouldn't want:
❌ «wouldn't want» belongs to the second conditional with would, not to the first.
Example: *I won't call him if I don't want to.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional with the modal verb must.
Formula: **must not + V1** (for example, «must not show»).
Explanation: The prohibition in the result of type 1 is conveyed through must not + base verb.
Example: *If you have the treasure map, you must not show it to us.*

a1.must not show:
✅ "must not show" — the correct modal form must not + V1 for the result of the first conditional.
Example: *If you have the treasure map, you must not show it to us.*
a1.will not show:
❌ "will not show" contains will, but here the prohibition must not is needed.
Example: *If you have the treasure map, you must not show it to us.*
a1.do not show:
❌ "do not show" — Present Simple without the modal verb must.
Example: *If you have the treasure map, you must not show it to us.*
a1.would not show:
❌ "would not show" belongs to the second conditional with would. Here we use must not.
Example: *If you have the treasure map, you must not show it to us.*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: We express a hypothetical situation in the second conditional using the Past Simple of the verb "win".
Example: *If you won the lottery, what would you buy?*

a1.won:
✅ "won" — the correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun "you".
Example: *If you won the lottery, what would you buy?*
a1.win:
❌ "win" — Present Simple, but the second condition requires Past Simple.
Example: *If you won the lottery, what would you buy?*
a1.will win:
❌ "will win" contains will, which is characteristic of the first conditional. Here Past Simple is needed.
Example: *If you won the lottery, what would you buy?*
a1.would win:
❌ "would win" — a result with would, not a part with if.
Example: *If you won the lottery, what would you buy?*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: We express a hypothetical situation in the second conditional using the Past Simple of the verb "be".
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **would + V1** (for example, "would do").
Explanation: In the result of the second conditional, we use would + base verb.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*

a1.were:
✅ "were" — the correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun "I".
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*
a1.was:
❌ "was" — the form was; in the second conditional for any subject we use were.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*
a1.am:
❌ "am" — Present Simple, but the second condition requires Past Simple.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*
a1.would be:
❌ "would be" — a result with would, not a part with if.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*

a2.would do:
✅ "would do" — the correct construction would + V1 for the result of the second conditional.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*
a2.will do:
❌ "will do" with will belongs to the first conditional, but here would is needed.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*
a2.did:
❌ "did" — Past Simple; we need would + V1.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*
a2.do:
❌ "do" — Present Simple; in the second conditional, we describe the result through would.
Example: *If I were you, I would do the same in your place.*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: We express a hypothetical situation in the second conditional using the Past Simple of the verb "not go".
Example: *If I didn't go to the cinema, I would be extremely sad.*

a1.didn't go:
✅ "didn't go" — the correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun "I".
Example: *If I didn't go to the cinema, I would be extremely sad.*
a1.don't go:
❌ "don't go" — Present Simple, but the second condition requires Past Simple.
Example: *If I didn't go to the cinema, I would be extremely sad.*
a1.won't go:
❌ "won't go" contains will, which is characteristic of the first conditional. Here Past Simple is needed.
Example: *If I didn't go to the cinema, I would be extremely sad.*
a1.hadn't gone:
❌ "hadn't gone" — the form of Third Conditional (had + V3). It is not used in the second conditional.
Example: *If I didn't go to the cinema, I would be extremely sad.*

hints:
Tense: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: An imaginary situation in the second conditional is conveyed using Past Simple of the verb "be".
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*
Tense: Second Conditional.
Formula: **would + V1** (for example, "would ask").
Explanation: In the result of the second conditional, we use would + base verb.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*

a1.were:
✅ "were" — the correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun "I".
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*
a1.was:
❌ "was" — the form was; in the second conditional, we use were for any subject.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*
a1.am:
❌ "am" — Present Simple, but the second conditional requires Past Simple.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*
a1.would be:
❌ "would be" — the result with would, not part of if.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*

a2.would ask:
✅ "would ask" — the correct construction would + V1 for the result of the second conditional.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*
a2.will ask:
❌ "will ask" with will belongs to the first conditional, but here we need would.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*
a2.ask:
❌ "ask" — Present Simple; in the second conditional, we describe the result using would.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*
a2.asked:
❌ "asked" — Past Simple; we need would + V1.
Example: *If I were too tired, I would ask her to go to the cinema with me.*

hints:
Tense: First Conditional.
Formula: **will + V1** (for example, "will be").
Explanation: The result in type 1 is expressed through will + base verb.
Example: *If my train isn't late, I will be in school on time.*

a1.will be:
✅ "will be" — the correct model will + V1 for the result of the first conditional.
Example: *If my train isn't late, I will be in school on time.*
a1.would be:
❌ "would be" uses would, and this is the second conditional. In type 1, we need will.
Example: *If my train isn't late, I will be in school on time.*
a1.am:
❌ "am" — Present Simple; the result must contain will.
Example: *If my train isn't late, I will be in school on time.*
a1.was:
❌ "was" — Past Simple, but we need will + base verb.
Example: *If my train isn't late, I will be in school on time.*

hints:
Tense: Second Conditional.
Formula: **would not / wouldn't + V1**.
Explanation: Negation in the second conditional is built using wouldn't + base verb "not be".
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*
Tense: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: An imaginary situation in the second conditional is conveyed using Past Simple of the verb "take".
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*

a1.wouldn't be:
✅ "wouldn't be" — the correct negation wouldn't + V1 in the second conditional.
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*
a1.won't be:
❌ "won't be" with won't corresponds to the first conditional, but in the second we need wouldn't.
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*
a1.aren't:
❌ "aren't" — Present Simple; an imaginary result requires wouldn't + V1.
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*
a1.weren't:
❌ "weren't" — Past Simple; in the second conditional, we need the form wouldn't + V1.
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*

a2.took:
✅ "took" — the correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun "you".
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*
a2.take:
❌ "take" — Present Simple, but the second conditional requires Past Simple.
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*
a2.will take:
❌ "will take" contains will, which is characteristic of the first conditional. Here, Past Simple is needed.
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*
a2.taken:
❌ "taken" — form of Third Conditional (had + V3). It is not used in the second conditional.
Example: *You wouldn't be so unfit if you took more exercise.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **will not / won't + V1**.
Explanation: For negation in the main sentence of type 1, we use won't + base verb "keep".
Example: *If he doesn't work harder, he won't keep his job.*

a1.won't keep:
✅ "won't keep" — correct negation won't + V1 for type 1 result.
Example: *If he doesn't work harder, he won't keep his job.*
a1.wouldn't keep:
❌ "wouldn't keep" has would, which is characteristic of the second conditional. Here, won't is needed.
Example: *If he doesn't work harder, he won't keep his job.*
a1.keeps:
❌ "keeps" — Present Simple without will; in the first conditional, the result is formed through won't.
Example: *If he doesn't work harder, he won't keep his job.*
a1.kept:
❌ "kept" — Past Simple; the correct negation is won't + V1.
Example: *If he doesn't work harder, he won't keep his job.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **will + V1** (for example, "will help").
Explanation: The result in type 1 is expressed through will + base verb.
Example: *If he doesn't pass the exam, will you help him?*

a1.help:
✅ "help" — correct model will + V1 for first conditional result.
Example: *If he doesn't pass the exam, will you help him?*
a1.will help: ❌ "will help" repeats will, although the auxiliary word is already in the sentence. See example: *If he doesn't pass the exam, will you help him?*
a1.helped:
❌ "helped" — Past Simple, but we need will + base verb.
Example: *If he doesn't pass the exam, will you help him?*
a1.would help:
❌ "would help" uses would, and this is the second condition. In type 1, will is needed.
Example: *If he doesn't pass the exam, will you help him?*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: The hypothetical situation in the second conditional is conveyed by Past Simple of the verb "not lose".
Example: *If he didn't lose his scholarship, his friends would visit him more often.*

a1.didn't lose:
✅ "didn't lose" — correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun "he".
Example: *If he didn't lose his scholarship, his friends would visit him more often.*
a1.doesn't lose:
❌ "doesn't lose" — Present Simple, but the second condition requires Past Simple.
Example: *If he didn't lose his scholarship, his friends would visit him more often.*
a1.wouldn't lose:
❌ "wouldn't lose" — result with would, not part with if.
Example: *If he didn't lose his scholarship, his friends would visit him more often.*
a1.had not lost:
❌ "had not lost" — form of Third Conditional (had + V3). It is not used in the second conditional.
Example: *If he didn't lose his scholarship, his friends would visit him more often.*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple**.
Explanation: The hypothetical situation in the second conditional is conveyed by Past Simple of the verb "not live".
Example: *If he didn't live so far, his friends would visit him every weekend.*

a1.didn't live:
✅ "didn't live" — correct form of Past Simple in the subordinate clause of the second conditional for the pronoun "he".
Example: *If he didn't live so far, his friends would visit him every weekend.*
a1.doesn't live:
❌ "doesn't live" — Present Simple, but the second condition requires Past Simple.
Example: *If he didn't live so far, his friends would visit him every weekend.*
a1.lived:
❌ Incorrect option.
Example: *If he didn't live so far, his friends would visit him every weekend.*
a1.wouldn't live:
❌ "wouldn't live" — result with would, not part with if.
Example: *If he didn't live so far, his friends would visit him every weekend.*
